Within Data Center, Nvidia is splitting revenue into two major sub-segments: Hyperscale, which includes hyperscale cloud providers and large internet companies (AWS, Google, Meta, Microsoft, etc.), and ACIE (AI Clouds, Industrial, and Enterprise), which covers enterprise AI factories, industrial deployments, sovereign AI deployments, supercomputing, and other deployments not controlled by hyperscalers. Edge Computing will include PCs, workstations, robotics, automotive, gaming consoles, and telecom infrastructure.Splitting its data center segment into two major sub-segments — hyperscalers and ACIE —makes a lot of sense for Nvidia. Hyperscalers tend to deploy custom silicon, own AI accelerators, or hardware from competitors, which limits Nvidia's growth in this segment. By contrast, there are thousands of participants in the broad ACIE category, virtually all of whom can take advantage of Nvidia's rack-scale platform-based approach, and almost none of them can afford development of custom silicon, which means limitless growth for Nvidia. As a result, Jensen Huang repeatedly emphasized that the ACIE category eventually becomes larger than hyperscale simply because AI is going to become ubiquitous and there are thousands of companies to address.The 'new' resultsAs Nvidia will from now on report its revenue split differently, the company had to re-report results across the new market platforms for previous physical years. One interesting observation is that the ACIE segment was outperforming the hyperscale segment till Q2 FY2026, when large CSPs began to deploy Nvidia's GB300 platform for inference, spending tens of billions of dollars per quarter. Now, the ACIE platform has almost caught up. (Image credit: Nvidia)In the first quarter of its fiscal 2027, hyperscalers bought $37.869 billion worth of hardware from Nvidia, revenue from the ACIE segment reached $37.377 billion, whereas sales of edge computing hardware totaled $6.369 billion. Meanwhile, as noted above, while the bulk of Nvidia's revenue today comes from sales of compute and networking hardware, the company also sells graphics processors, but going forward, those sales will be reported as part of other segments. For example, sales of graphics hardware topped $7.065 billion in Q1 FY2027, which by far exceeds sales of the edge computing segment and strongly suggests that Nvidia now considers graphics as part of multiple newly established market platforms.Stay On the Cutting Edge: Get the Tom's Hardware NewsletterGet Tom's Hardware's best news and in-depth reviews, straight to your inbox.Contact me with news and offers from other Future brandsReceive email from us on behalf of our trusted partners or sponsorsOutlook: $100 billion per quarter approachingFor the second quarter of fiscal 2027, Nvidia expects revenue of about $91 billion ± 2%. The company does not expect to ship any AI hardware to China. Gross margins are projected at around 74.9% on a GAAP basis. Nvidia expects GAAP operating expenses of roughly $8.5 billion. Follow Tom's Hardware on Google News, or add us as a preferred source, to get our latest news, analysis, & reviews in your feeds.
history.author: Anton Shilov
history.titleZh: 英伟达不再单独报告游戏GPU销售额——受AI热潮推动，第一季度利润达惊人的816亿美元
history.descriptionZh:
英伟达于周三公布其2027财年第一季度财务业绩，实现创纪录的816.15亿美元营收，较上一季度增长20%，同比飙升85%，创下公司史上最佳季度表现。净利润高达583.21亿美元，同比增幅达211%，毛利率维持在74.9%的高位。其中，计算与网络硬件业务销售额达到745.5亿美元（历史新高），图形硬件销售额为70.65亿美元，环比增长20%，同比增长85%。这一成绩凸显了人工智能硬件需求的爆炸性增长，但也促使英伟达彻底调整其财务报告架构。

在看似辉煌的数字背后，一个更为深刻的转变是：英伟达宣布将不再单独报告游戏和专业显卡的销售数据。这意味着其传统的“消费者GPU”类别将不复存在，取而代之的是按部署市场划分的全新平台报告框架。此举明确标志着英伟达的核心业务已彻底从图形处理器转向人工智能与数据中心基础设施。新的报告结构分为两大平台：**数据中心**和**边缘计算**。其中，数据中心进一步细分为两个子板块：
- **超大规模（Hyperscale）**：涵盖AWS、Google、Meta、Microsoft等大型云服务提供商和互联网巨头。
- **ACIE（AI云、工业与企业）**：包括企业AI工厂、工业部署、主权AI部署、超级计算中心以及其他非超大规模控制方所部署的算力。

边缘计算则涵盖PC、工作站、机器人、汽车、游戏主机及电信基础设施。这一划分策略极具深意：超大规模客户往往采用自研芯片（如TPU、Trainium）或竞争对手的产品，这限制了英伟达在该领域的增长天花板；而ACIE领域涉及成千上万家企业，几乎都无法承担自研芯片的巨额成本，且大多依赖英伟达的机架级平台方案（如DGX系列、GB300等），因此被视为“无限增长”的蓝海。英伟达CEO黄仁勋多次强调，随着AI变得无处不在，ACIE市场规模终将超越超大规模市场。

为了适应新框架，英伟达还重新报告了往期财务数据。有趣的是，在2026财年第二季度之前，ACIE板块的表现一直优于超大规模板块；但此后，大型云服务商开始大规模部署英伟达的GB300推理平台，每个季度投入数百亿美元，导致超大规模板块反超。然而到2027财年第一季度，两者已几乎持平：超大规模客户贡献营收378.69亿美元，ACIE板块营收373.77亿美元，边缘计算硬件销售额为63.69亿美元。值得注意的是，图形硬件销售额（70.65亿美元）远超边缘计算板块的营收，这表明英伟达已将图形处理器业务分散归入多个新设立的细分市场中，不再作为独立类别存在。

从技术原理与创新点来看，英伟达的成功不仅源于GPU本身的算力，更在于其从芯片到系统再到软件的全栈解决方案。例如，基于Blackwell架构的GB200/GB300平台将GPU、CPU、网络交换机和高带宽内存紧密集成，形成可规模化部署的AI工厂。在推理侧，英伟达的TensorRT推理引擎与NVLink互联技术大幅降低了大规模模型部署的延迟和成本，这正是超大规模客户愿意持续投入的核心原因。此外，英伟达通过CUDA生态锁定开发者，使得ACIE领域的企业难以转向其他硬件平台。

性能参数与对比数据方面：本季度营收816亿美元，是去年同期（约441亿美元）的1.85倍；净利润同比增长211%；毛利率稳定在74.9%，显示出强大的定价能力和成本控制。超大规模与ACIE板块几乎贡献等量营收，说明AI算力需求正在从少数巨头向广泛的企业和政府市场渗透。边缘计算虽然体量尚小，但63.69亿美元的季度收入已相当于一家中型半导体公司的全年营收。

技术影响与应用场景：英伟达的全新报告框架实质上反映了AI基础设施市场正在分化为两大阶层——超级云计算供应商（控制大型模型训练与云端推理）和广泛的行业/企业/主权AI部署（包括工业质检、自动驾驶、医疗影像、金融风控、人形机器人等）。后者几乎完全依赖英伟达的预制化方案，无法像超大规模公司那样自研芯片。这意味着英伟达在ACIE领域几乎不存在竞争对手，增长潜力巨大。同时，边缘计算板块覆盖了智能汽车（Orin/Thor芯片）、机器人（Isaac平台）、游戏主机等场景，未来随着AI从云端走向终端，该板块也将显著增长。

展望未来，英伟达预计2027财年第二季度营收约为910亿美元（±2%），并重申不会向中国出货任何AI硬件。GAAP毛利率预期仍维持在74.9%左右，运营费用约85亿美元。按此趋势，英伟达单季度营收跨越千亿美元大关可能就在数季度之内。这一消息引发行业震动：它意味着全球AI算力投资的绝对主力依然是英伟达，而公司已彻底从“显卡公司”转型为“AI基础设施公司”，其财务报告方式自身就是行业变迁的缩影。

history.title: In SpaceX’s IPO, Elon Musk is the risk factor
history.description:
The SpaceX IPO is here, and it's more than just an historic public offering that could make Elon Musk the world's first trillionaire. It also reveals more ways in which Elon Musk's companies interact and overlap with each other, shuffling money around in ways that are often difficult to keep track of. 
This is evident in ways that are both obvious and less so. A CTRL-F search for "Tesla" yields 87 results, "xAI" is mentioned 356 times, "Grok" 243 times, and "X" 267 times. Even the "Boring Company" (7 times), "Neuralink" (3), and "Optimus" (1) get a few mentions. Throughout its 330 pages of rocket launches and interplanetary wishes, you can  …
Read the full story at The Verge.
history.content: The SpaceX IPO is here, and it’s more than just an historic public offering that could make Elon Musk the world’s first trillionaire. It also reveals more ways in which Elon Musk’s companies interact and overlap with each other, shuffling money around in ways that are often difficult to keep track of.This is evident in ways that are both obvious and less so. A CTRL-F search for “Tesla” yields 87 results, “xAI” is mentioned 356 times, “Grok” 243 times, and “X” 267 times. Even the “Boring Company” (7 times), “Neuralink” (3), and “Optimus” (1) get a few mentions. Throughout its 330 pages of rocket launches and interplanetary wishes, you can trace the network of ways in which Musk’s companies deal with each other.It’s also evident in the ways Musk’s companies are shareholders in other Musk companies, further intertwining their fates in the process. Based on the Form S-1 filing, Tesla owns nearly 19 million shares of SpaceX’s Class A common stock, which is less than 1 percent of the total outstanding stock. Tesla’s stake in xAI was converted to SpaceX shares after Elon Musk merged his AI company with his space company in February.The filing also reveals SpaceX bought $131 million worth of Cybertrucks “at manufacturer’s suggested retail price from Tesla.” A Bloomberg report earlier this year suggested that SpaceX bought 1,279 Cybertrucks in the fourth quarter of 2025, but the IPO suggests it has probably acquired a few more than that. As Electrek notes, without these purchases, Cybertruck registration numbers likely would have gone down year over year.Tesla’s Megapacks, the company’s giant stationary storage batteries, are used to stabilize SpaceX’s Colossus I and II data centers in Memphis, TN, during peak demand. The rocket company purchased $697 million worth of Megapacks from Tesla in 2024 and 2025.RelatedThe SpaceX IPO is a trillion-dollar gamble on the future of spaceSpaceX’s relationship with Musk’s Boring Company is much more quaint in comparison. The tunneling venture has paid about $1.2 million in office leases to SpaceX. And SpaceX spent about $1 million for the Boring Company to dig a tunnel at its headquarters in Bastrop, Texas.SpaceX was valued at $1.25 trillion earlier this year after merging with xAI, Musk’s AI company that also owns X, formerly Twitter. The tie-up means investors will be buying in at a historically high price — but Musk combined the companies at great cost to himself and also SpaceX. The filing showed that the rocket company directed about 60 percent of its capital spending in 2025 toward xAI, or about $20 billion. But as TechCrunch notes, xAI lost billions of dollars last year on revenue that grew by only 22 percent year over year.When going public, companies are required to list their risk factors, under the assumption that investors should know about all the skeletons in the closet before putting their money down. For SpaceX, the biggest risk is also the biggest asset: Elon Musk.For SpaceX, the biggest risk is also the biggest asset: Elon MuskWhile any company, especially one as complex as SpaceX, would be expected to include a long list of risk factors in its S-1, SpaceX’s is unique in that it includes its own CEO. The filing explicitly states that SpaceX is “highly dependent on the continued services of Mr. Musk,” noting that his leadership, vision, and technical expertise are critical to the company’s future.Like other Musk-owned companies, SpaceX acknowledges that Musk isn’t always 100 percent focused on SpaceX. And it admits that Musk’s intersecting businesses may end up cannibalizing each other in some way. Conflicts could arise. And if they do, Musk is not “restricted” from doing something that directly competes with his other companies, including SpaceX.Conflicts of interest could arise in the future between us, on the one hand, and Mr. Musk and entities owned by or affiliated with him, on the other hand, concerning among other things, business transactions, potential competitive business activities or other opportunities…. Furthermore, Mr. Musk and other businesses owned by or affiliated with him may now, or in the future, directly or indirectly, compete with us for investment or business opportunities.The S-1 goes on to enumerate the ways in which Musk’s extensive entanglements could result in financial loss for SpaceX. The company is completely dependent on his leadership, and yet could also incur big losses as a result of said leadership. (See: Tesla in 2025.)For instance, Mr. Musk currently serves as Technoking and Chief Executive Officer of Tesla and is involved in other emerging technology ventures, including Neuralink and The Boring Company. Mr. Musk has also previously served as Senior Advisor to the President of the United States. Any such loss or reduced involvement in our business could result in a material adverse effect on our business, financial condition, results of operations, and future prospects.The pull between risk and reward is a running theme throughout the filing.We, Mr. Musk, and other companies Mr. Musk is affiliated with frequently receive an immense amount of media attention. The actions and statements of Mr. Musk and his affiliated ventures, whether or not directly relating to us, may draw significant public attention and scrutiny to us and could potentially have a positive or negative impact on our business, relationships with customers and regulators, or stock price.These are not statements you find in your average S-1 filing, but SpaceX is not your typical IPO. Musk stands to make billions if SpaceX establishes a “permanent” colony on Mars with “at least” a million inhabitants. He’s also a shit magnet that could do serious damage to SpaceX’s reputation. Musk’s companies do business with and are deeply entangled with each other in ways laid bare by the filing. They buy each other’s stuff and compete with each other for RAM, AI chips, and other ultra-valuable components that are increasingly in short supply.Occasionally, his shareholders push back. In 2024, several Tesla shareholders sued Musk over claims he was knowingly diverting talent and resources away from the company and directing them toward xAI. That lawsuit is still pending.
history.author: Andrew J. Hawkins
history.titleZh: 在SpaceX的IPO中，埃隆·马斯克是风险因素
history.descriptionZh:
SpaceX的IPO终于来了，这不仅仅是一次历史性的公开募股——这笔交易有可能让埃隆·马斯克成为全球首位万亿富翁，同时也以更为复杂的方式揭示了其旗下各公司之间如何相互交织、频繁转移资金，其路径往往令人难以追踪。这种关联体现在明处和暗处。在SpaceX长达330页的招股说明书中，搜索“特斯拉”出现87次，“xAI”出现356次，“Grok”243次，“X”267次，就连“无聊公司”也有7次，“Neuralink”3次，“Optimus”1次。透过这些数字，可以勾勒出一张马斯克旗下公司彼此交易的网络。

更深刻的纠缠在于股权层面。根据S-1文件，特斯拉持有SpaceX约1900万股A类普通股，虽然占比不到总股本的1%，但这是一份实实在在的交叉持股。今年2月，马斯克将其AI公司xAI与SpaceX合并后，特斯拉在xAI的股份也被转换成了SpaceX股票。这种结构让各家公司的命运进一步捆绑在一起。

除了股权，还有真金白银的采购。SpaceX以“制造商量建议零售价”从特斯拉购买了价值1.31亿美元的Cybertruck。彭博社今年早些时候曾报道，SpaceX在2025年第四季度购买了1279辆Cybertruck，但招股书暗示实际数量可能更多。如科技媒体Electrek所指出的，如果没有这些内部采购，Cybertruck的注册量可能会同比下滑。更重要的采购是特斯拉的Megapack——该公司的大型固定式储能电池。SpaceX用这些电池来稳定其位于田纳西州孟菲斯的Colossus I和II数据中心在峰值用电时的电网负荷。仅在2024和2025年两年，SpaceX就向特斯拉采购了价值6.97亿美元的Megapack。

相比之下，SpaceX与马斯克另一家公司“无聊公司”的交易规模要小得多，也更朴素。无聊公司向SpaceX支付了约120万美元的办公室租金，而SpaceX花费约100万美元请无聊公司在德州巴斯特罗普的总部挖了一条隧道。

SpaceX在今年早些时候与xAI合并后估值达到1.25万亿美元。合并意味着投资者将在一个历史高位买入，但马斯克本人和SpaceX也为这次合并付出了巨大代价。文件显示，2025年SpaceX约60%的资本支出（约200亿美元）流向了xAI。然而，据TechCrunch统计，xAI去年仍在亏损，且营收同比增长仅为22%。

任何公司上市时都需要列出风险因素，让投资者在掏钱前了解所有“衣橱里的骷髅”。对SpaceX来说，最大的风险同时也是最大的资产：埃隆·马斯克。

文件明确指出，SpaceX“高度依赖马斯克先生的持续服务”，强调他的领导力、远见和技术专长对公司的未来至关重要。同时，SpaceX承认，马斯克并非总是100%专注于SpaceX，他旗下相互交织的公司可能会在某种程度上彼此吞噬。“未来我们与马斯克先生及其关联实体之间可能会在商业交易、潜在竞争性业务活动或其他机会方面产生利益冲突……此外，马斯克先生及其关联业务现在或将来可能直接或间接地与我们在投资或商业机会上竞争。”这些措辞在普通的S-1文件中很少见到。

更具体地，S-1列举了马斯克广泛涉足所带来的风险：“马斯克先生目前担任特斯拉的Technoking（电音之王）和首席执行官，并参与其他新兴科技企业，包括Neuralink和无聊公司。马斯克先生还曾担任美国总统的高级顾问。他任何形式的离职或减少参与都可能对我们的业务、财务状况、经营成果和未来前景产生重大不利影响。”

风险与回报之间的拉扯贯穿整个文件。“我们、马斯克先生以及马斯克先生关联的其他公司经常受到媒体的广泛关注。马斯克先生及其关联企业的行为和言论，无论是否直接与我们相关，都可能给我们带来公众关注和审视，并可能对我们的业务、与客户和监管机构的关系或股价产生正面或负面影响。”

马斯克的公司在彼此做生意，同时也互相争夺RAM、AI芯片等日益稀缺的高价值组件。2024年，多名特斯拉股东起诉马斯克，指控他故意将特斯拉的人才和资源转投给xAI，该诉讼目前仍在审理中。SpaceX的IPO说到底是一场关于火星殖民和万亿赌注的未来叙事，而这一切都建立在马斯克这个既是天才又是不稳定因素的人物之上。

history.title: AMD Ryzen AI Max 400 ‘Gorgon Halo’ packs up to 192GB of unified memory — refreshed APU uses Zen 5 and RDNA 3.5, and can clock up to 5.2 GHz
history.description: AMD is refreshing its Ryzen AI Max chips with the new 400 series, known as Gorgon Halo, and they’ll show up in the Ryzen AI Halo development box. That box, with last-gen Strix Halo chips, is going up for pre-order in June, starting at $3,999.
history.content: Copy link Facebook X Whatsapp Reddit Pinterest Flipboard Email Share this article 8 Join the conversation Follow us Add us as a preferred source on Google Newsletter Subscribe to our newsletter AMD is refreshing its stack of large SoCs, dubbed Ryzen AI Max, with new Gorgon Point chips. Codenamed Gorgon Halo, the Ryzen AI Max 400 range is a minor refresh to the Ryzen AI Max 300 ‘Strix Halo’ chips already available, similar to what we saw with Gorgon Point in laptops earlier this year. Gorgon Halo comes with one significant difference, however, which is space for up to 192GB of unified memory.Go deeper with TH Premium: CPU (Image credit: Tom's Hardware)CPU scaling with DLSSRyzen to the top: How AMD innovated in the gaming CPU marketHow ARM is working its way into PCsAMD CES 2026 gaming trends press Q&A roundtable transcriptWith Strix Halo, you could pack up to 128GB of unified memory, but AMD is pushing that boundary higher with Gorgon Halo; perhaps at the worst possible time, as global DRAM shortages continue to push prices up across all business categories. It’ll be a minor miracle if AMD is able to actually ship Gorgon Point with 192GB of unified memory consistently — we’ve seen Apple remove the 512GB option and even the 128GB option from the Mac Studio due to memory shortages.Regardless, AMD has a lineup of three chips that should look very familiar if you’ve looked over the Strix Halo stack. All three chips use Zen 5 CPU cores and RDNA 3.5 GPU cores, alongside an XDNA 2 NPU. The flagship Ryzen AI Max+ Pro 495 comes with a minor clock speed bump of 100 MHz over the Ryzen AI Max+ 395, allowing it to boost to 5.2 GHz. Otherwise, you could scratch off the “4” and replace it with a “3,” at least based on the specs AMD has shared so far.Latest Videos From You may like AMD Ryzen AI Max+ PRO 495 APU could arrive with 192GB of unified memory AMD details Ryzen AI 400 desktop with up to 8 cores, Radeon 860M graphics AMD Ryzen AI 5 435G APU goes head-to-head with the Ryzen 5 8600G in early benchmarks Swipe to scroll horizontallyRow 0 - Cell 0 Cores / ThreadsArch (CPU / GPU)Boost ClockTotal CacheNPU TOPSiGPU (CUs)Unified memory (GPU memory)Max+ Pro 49516 / 32Zen 5 / RDNA 3.55.2 GHz80 MB55Radeon 8065S (40)Up to 192 GB (160 GB)Max Pro 49012 / 24Zen 5 / RDNA 3.55 GHz76 MB50Radeon 8050S (32)Up to 192 GB (160 GB)Max Pro 4858 / 16Zen 5 / RDNA 3.55 GHz40 MB50Radeon 8050S (32)Up to 192 GB (160 GB)These chips currently have a “Pro” tag, which means they’re targeting the commercial market. However, the slides below refer to the Ryzen AI Max 400 range more broadly. I asked AMD about this discrepancy, and a spokesperson sent the following: “AMD will be announcing the Ryzen AI Max PRO 400 Series, featuring AMD PRO technologies which deliver enterprise-grade security, manageability, and reliability.”So, I guess consumer Gorgon Halo is still up in the air. Maybe.Interestingly, AMD opted to stick with a GPU with 32 CUs (the Radeon 8050S) for the Ryzen AI Max Pro 490 and 485. Earlier this year, AMD refreshed the Ryzen AI Max 385 and 390 with 40 CUs, the same as the flagship, in the form of the Ryzen AI Max+ 388 and 392. Maybe we’ll see a refresh of the refreshed Gorgon Halo chips with 40 CUs down the line.Image 1 of 4(Image credit: AMD)(Image credit: AMD)(Image credit: AMD)(Image credit: AMD)Memory is the big upgrade here. Regardless of the GPU configuration, up to 160GB of unified memory can function as VRAM (32GB is reserved for the system). AMD says that much memory makes Ryzen AI Max 400 chips the first x86 client processors able to run a 300B+ parameter LLM. It wins in a category of one, however: Intel doesn’t make a large SoC like Gorgon Halo, and Apple uses the ARM ISA.Stay On the Cutting Edge: Get the Tom's Hardware NewsletterGet Tom's Hardware's best news and in-depth reviews, straight to your inbox.Contact me with news and offers from other Future brandsReceive email from us on behalf of our trusted partners or sponsorsAMD says Ryzen AI Max 400 chips are “coming soon,” but didn’t share any timeline beyond that, nor any partners for Gorgon Halo systems. Strix Halo, as a niche product, was only available in a handful of machines, such as the Framework Desktop, ROG Flow Z13, and GMKtec EVO-X2. There’s a good chance we’ll see a similarly conservative rollout of Gorgon Halo, as well.Despite not sharing any partners, AMD tells me that “several OEM partners have expressed excitement for the Ryzen AI Halo platform and the Ryzen AI Max Pro 400 series family of processors,” and that “systems will be announced from our partners starting in Q3 2026.”AMD Ryzen AI Halo starts at $3,999 — pre-orders in JuneThe only confirmed machine with Ryzen AI Max 400 so far is the Ryzen AI Halo, which is “coming soon” configured with the Ryzen AI Max+ Pro 495. Coming sooner is the Ryzen AI Halo box AMD revealed earlier this year with the Ryzen AI Max+ 395. AMD is opening up pre-orders in June, and it says the machine starts at $3,999. What to read next Corsair's Strix Halo AI Workstation 300 gets even more expensive amid the RAMpocalypse AMD Ryzen 9 9950X3D2 unveiled as first dual-cache X3D CPU Minisforum's beastly N5 Max NAS comes powered by AMD Strix Halo (Image credit: AMD)The starting configuration includes a Ryzen AI Max+ 395 with 128GB of unified memory and 2TB of storage. It says “details and pricing of the other configurations will be released closer to on-shelf,” so it sounds like we’ll see additional models in the future. The Ryzen AI Halo’s main competitor, Nvidia’s DGX Spark, is currently selling for $4,700 with 128GB of unified memory, Nvidia’s GB10 chip, and 4TB of storage. (Image credit: AMD)The Ryzen AI Halo supports Linux and Windows, while the DGX Spark is limited to Linux. Still, the penguin seems like the primary platform for this box. With Linux, AMD says the Ryzen AI Halo offers up to 14% higher tokens per second than the DGX Spark with the GLM 4.7 Flash 30B model, as well as up to 4% higher tokens per second with Qwen 3.6 35B. AMD also compared the Ryzen AI Halo to the Mac Mini M4 Pro, showing around 4X scaling in AI workloads. That’s not really a fair comparison, however; a Mac Studio is more akin to the level of compute inside the Ryzen AI Halo or DGX Spark. (Image credit: AMD)Outside of the core components, the Ryzen AI Halo comes with Wi-Fi 7, Bluetooth 5.4, and 10Gbps Ethernet, along with an HDMI 2.1b display output. The device includes three USB-C ports (no word on speeds yet), along with a fourth USB-C used for power delivery. The rated TDP is up to 120W for the box. It’s a lot of hardware crammed into a tiny space, with the Ryzen AI Halo coming in at 5.9 x 5.9 x 1.7 inches.Although these boxes are expensive, AMD is framing the Ryzen AI Halo around the “token economy,” similar to how Nvidia has messaged against its data center hardware. AMD says one Ryzen AI Halo box can save up to $750 each month over using cloud compute, claiming the Ryzen AI Halo will break even on cost after six months (assuming six million tokens per day). With AI agents, that token usage is certainly possible. Just this month, we saw OpenClaw developer Peter Steinberger rack up $1.3 million in OpenAI API usage in just 30 days across a three-person team working on the agentic AI framework.Pre-orders open in June for the Ryzen AI Halo., although AMD hasn’t shared the exact date. For the updated Ryzen AI Halo box with Gorgon Halo chips, AMD hasn’t announced any release date yet. Assuming we see more systems in Q3 as AMD has suggested, we should have a better idea about the Gorgon Halo rollout at that point.Full presentationImage 1 of 39(Image credit: AMD)(Image credit: AMD)(Image credit: AMD)(Image credit: AMD)(Image credit: AMD)(Image credit: AMD)(Image credit: AMD)(Image credit: AMD)(Image credit: AMD)(Image credit: AMD)(Image credit: AMD)(Image credit: AMD)(Image credit: AMD)(Image credit: AMD)(Image credit: AMD)(Image credit: AMD)(Image credit: AMD)(Image credit: AMD)(Image credit: AMD)(Image credit: AMD)(Image credit: AMD)(Image credit: AMD)(Image credit: AMD)(Image credit: AMD)(Image credit: AMD)(Image credit: AMD)(Image credit: AMD)(Image credit: AMD)(Image credit: AMD)(Image credit: AMD)(Image credit: AMD)(Image credit: AMD)(Image credit: AMD)(Image credit: AMD)(Image credit: AMD)(Image credit: AMD)(Image credit: AMD)(Image credit: AMD)(Image credit: AMD) Follow Tom's Hardware on Google News, or add us as a preferred source, to get our latest news, analysis, & reviews in your feeds.
history.author: Jake Roach
history.titleZh: AMD Ryzen AI Max 400 ‘Gorgon Halo’拥有高达192GB统一内存——刷新版APU采用Zen 5和RDNA 3.5，频率可达5.2 GHz
history.descriptionZh:
AMD正式推出其大型SoC产品线的升级版本——Ryzen AI Max 400系列（代号Gorgon Halo），这是对现有Ryzen AI Max 300系列（Strix Halo）的常规迭代更新。与年初笔记本平台上的Gorgon Point升级类似，此次Gorgon Halo同样属于小改款，但最显著的变化在于统一内存容量上限从上一代的128GB跃升至192GB。这一升级恰逢全球DRAM短缺导致内存价格持续上涨的敏感时期，AMD能否稳定供货192GB版本尚存悬念——此前苹果就因内存短缺被迫取消了Mac Studio的512GB甚至128GB选项。

Gorgon Halo系列共推出三款芯片，与Strix Halo的布局高度相似。所有型号均采用Zen 5 CPU核心、RDNA 3.5 GPU核心以及XDNA 2 NPU。旗舰型号Ryzen AI Max+ Pro 495相对于上一代旗舰Ryzen AI Max+ 395，仅在最高加速频率上提升了100MHz，达到5.2GHz。其他两款为：Ryzen AI Max Pro 490（12核24线程，5.0GHz，Radeon 8050S 32CU GPU）和Ryzen AI Max Pro 485（8核16线程，5.0GHz，Radeon 8050S 32CU GPU）。值得注意的是，AMD这次为Pro 490和485均配备了32CU的GPU（型号Radeon 8050S），而未像此前在Strix Halo中期更新中那样为中端型号升级至40CU。这可能意味着未来还会有40CU版本的Gorgon Halo芯片出现。

内存是本次升级的核心亮点。三款芯片均支持最高192GB统一内存，其中最多160GB可作为显存（VRAM）使用，剩余32GB保留给系统。AMD宣称，这使得Ryzen AI Max 400系列成为首款能够运行超过300B参数大语言模型（LLM）的x86客户端处理器。在同类产品中，Intel没有类似的大型SoC与Gorgon Halo竞争，而Apple则采用ARM指令集架构，因此AMD在这一细分市场尚无直接对手。

Ryzen AI Max 400系列目前均带有“Pro”标识，明确瞄准商业市场。AMD发言人确认：“AMD将发布Ryzen AI Max PRO 400系列，集成了AMD PRO技术，提供企业级安全、可管理性和可靠性。”这意味着消费版Gorgon Halo（非Pro）的发布时间尚未确定。

AMD表示Ryzen AI Max 400系列“即将推出”，但未给出具体时间表，也未公布任何合作伙伴的整机方案。前代Strix Halo作为一款小众产品，仅出现在少数设备中，如Framework Desktop、ROG Flow Z13和GMKtec EVO-X2，预计Gorgon Halo的推广节奏也将同样保守。不过AMD透露，多家OEM合作伙伴已对Ryzen AI Halo平台和Ryzen AI Max Pro 400系列表示兴趣，相关系统预计将从2026年第三季度开始发布。

目前唯一确认搭载Ryzen AI Max 400的成品是AMD自家的Ryzen AI Halo迷你主机（配置Ryzen AI Max+ Pro 495），而此前搭载Ryzen AI Max+ 395的版本已于年初亮相。AMD将于6月开启Ryzen AI Halo的预购，起售价为3999美元。基础配置包括Ryzen AI Max+ 395、128GB统一内存和2TB存储。未来将有更多配置选项，定价和详细规格将在临近上市时公布。

AMD将Ryzen AI Halo的主要竞争对手定为Nvidia的DGX Spark（售价4700美元，配备128GB统一内存、Nvidia GB10芯片和4TB存储）。在AI推理性能方面，AMD展示了对比数据：运行GLM 4.7 Flash 30B模型时，Ryzen AI Halo（基于Linux）的每秒token数比DGX Spark高出最多14%；运行Qwen 3.6 35B模型时则高出最多4%。此外，与Mac Mini M4 Pro相比，Ryzen AI Halo在AI工作负载上展现出约4倍的性能扩展。但AMD也承认这一比较并不完全公平，Mac Studio才更符合Ryzen AI Halo或DGX Spark的定位。

硬件配置方面，Ryzen AI Halo支持Wi-Fi 7、蓝牙5.4和10Gbps以太网，配备HDMI 2.1b显示输出、三个USB-C接口（速率未公布）以及一个用于供电的USB-C接口。整机功耗高达120W，体积为5.9×5.9×1.7英寸，在紧凑空间内集成了大量硬件。

AMD围绕“Token经济”概念推销Ryzen AI Halo，声称一台设备每月可节省高达750美元的云端计算费用，假设每日处理600万token，六个月即可收回成本。鉴于AI Agent正在消耗大量API调用（例如OpenClaw开发者团队一个月内就产生了130万美元的OpenAI API费用），这一使用场景确实存在。预购将于6月开始，但搭载Gorgon Halo芯片的更新版Ryzen AI Halo尚未公布发布日期，预计随着Q3 OEM系统上市，相关信息会更加明确。
